feature_name: Dual operation modes (RC and EP)
description: The PCIe module supports dual operation modes: Root Complex (RC) and End Point (EP). Mode selection is controlled by bootstrap pins PCIESSMODE[1:0] at power-up and can be overridden in software. In EP mode it can operate as a legacy or native PCIe end point. The core integrates Transaction, Data Link, and MAC layers to realize both roles. This flexibility enables the device to either host downstream devices or present itself as a peripheral to a host.
dependencies: Bootstrap PCIESSMODE[1:0]; DEVSTAT register to overwrite; LTSSM enable
Remarks: Ensure correct mode before link training and enumeration; changing mode at runtime requires full reinitialization.

feature_name: Single link, ×1/×2 lanes with dynamic width and lane reversal
description: The controller provides a single bidirectional PCIe link that can be configured for ×1 or ×2 lane operation. It supports dynamic-width conversion and automatic lane reversal to aid board routing and interoperability. Polarity inversion on receive is also supported for signal flexibility. Only one link is present; it cannot be split into two ×1 links or used as a switch. These characteristics define the top-level link topology and capabilities.
Remarks: Plan PCB lane assignments with lane reversal support; do not attempt multi-port configurations.

feature_name: Gen1/Gen2 link speeds (2.5/5.0 GT/s)
description: The PHY and core operate at 2.5 GT/s (Gen1) and 5.0 GT/s (Gen2) per lane. Gen2 operation is enabled by a directed speed change after initial Gen1 link up. The output PLL frequency remains 2.5 GHz with internal bus width conversion for Gen2. Software controls de-emphasis, swing, and speed change via port logic registers. These controls enable compliance tuning and performance scaling.
Remarks: After link up at Gen1, set DIR_SPD to initiate Gen2; ensure link partner supports Gen2 and common clock settings if applicable.

feature_name: Address Space 0 mapping (Registers/Config/Remote windows)
description: Address Space 0 is a contiguous 16 KB region partitioned into four 4 KB windows. It hosts application registers, local configuration space (Type 0/1), remote configuration access window, and a remote I/O window (RC only). These regions are non-burst and must be accessed with 32-bit transactions. Mapping enables local and remote PCIe management from the internal bus.
evidence: 2.8.1 Address Space 0; Figure 2-5; 2.8.1.2 Organization of Configuration Registers
dependencies: Internal bus aperture; IOBASE register for remote I/O; CFG_SETUP for config
Remarks: Mark these regions as non-cacheable and avoid burst accesses; guard remote windows when link is down.

feature_name: Outbound address translation (32 regions)
description: Outbound translation maps internal bus addresses to PCIe memory addresses for TLP generation. The memory-mapped outbound space is divided into 32 equal regions of 1/2/4/8 MB set by OB_SIZE. Each region’s PCIe base is programmed via OB_OFFSET_INDEXn (low) and OB_OFFSETn_HI (high for 64-bit). Translation occurs per request and does not span region boundaries, requiring size-aware DMA segments.
evidence: 2.7.1 Outbound Address Translation; 3.1.11 OB_SIZE; 3.1.80 OB_OFFSET_INDEXn; 3.1.81 OB_OFFSETn_HI
dependencies: Enable CMD_STATUS[OB_XLT_EN]; program OB_SIZE/OB_OFFSET*; DMA on internal slave port
Remarks: Segment large transfers to avoid crossing region limits; use 64-bit offsets for >4 GB addresses.

feature_name: Inbound address translation (4 regions) tied to BARs
description: Inbound translation remaps accepted PCIe accesses into internal memory. Four regions (0–3) are associated with BARs (BAR1–BAR5 for 32-bit; paired in 64-bit), defined by IB_BARn, IB_STARTn_{LO,HI}, and IB_OFFSETn. BAR0 is reserved for application registers and cannot be remapped. A special mode allows IB_START to derive from BAR1 when start registers are zero, simplifying EP windows.
evidence: 2.7.2 Inbound Address Translation; 2.7.2.2 BAR0 Exception; 2.7.2.3 Using BAR1 Value As Start; 3.1.82–3.1.97 IB_*
dependencies: Enable CMD_STATUS[IB_XLT_EN]; proper BAR setup; RC enumeration; address decode
Remarks: Define non-overlapping inbound windows; remember no inbound I/O is supported; document host-visible BAR sizes.

feature_name: BAR Mask registers and DBI_CS2 overlay
description: Before enumeration, software can configure EP BAR sizes, types, and enable states using BAR Mask registers overlaid on the BARs. Writes are allowed only when CMD_STATUS[DBI_CS2] is set, and reads reflect BAR, not mask contents. This mechanism lets firmware request specific aperture sizes from RC. BAR0 mask is limited (BAR0 dedicated to application registers).
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[DBI_CS2]; BAR*_MASK; clear DBI_CS2 before RC enumerates
Remarks: After writing mask, verify by test-write to BAR and readback; never modify via serial link; restrict BAR0 mask to allowed bits.

feature_name: Endian mode conversion (big/little with swizzle)
description: While the PCIe core is little-endian at the pins, the subsystem supports big-endian systems using configurable byte-swapping. The ENDIAN register selects swizzle granularity for inbound and outbound data paths. Tables define correct mapping for 1-, 2-, 4-, and 8-byte swaps. Proper configuration ensures data coherency across mixed-endian systems.
dependencies: ENDIAN[ENDIAN_MODE]; SoC boot endian mode; alignment rules
Remarks: Match ENDIAN mode to system ABI; verify with known patterns; note 32-bit MMR accesses swap on 4-byte boundaries.

feature_name: DMA support via external EDMA/internal ports
description: The PCIESS has no built-in DMA engine but exposes a 128-bit slave and a 128-bit master port on the internal interconnect. In RC mode, an external DMA reads/writes the PCIe window; in EP mode, remote bus masters target the master port. EDMA examples demonstrate 128-byte payload segmenting. Bandwidth targets are ~85% of 2.0/4.0 Gbps effective per lane for Gen1/Gen2, respectively.
dependencies: EDMA or SoC DMA; properly configured outbound/inbound translation; bus mastering enabled
Remarks: Size transfers to max payload/request; ensure byte strobe requirements and 128-byte boundary handling are satisfied.

feature_name: Legacy and MSI interrupt generation in EP
description: An EP can generate legacy INTx (A–D) or MSI, but not both simultaneously per PCIe rules. Legacy asserts are requested via EP_IRQ_SET/CLR, and MSI by issuing a memory write to the MSI address/data configured by RC. The MSI_IRQ register in the RC (or peer EP) is the target for vectored writes. Status and enable/clear registers exist per MSI group.
evidence: 2.14.2 Interrupt Generation in EP Mode; 3.1.16 MSI_IRQ; 3.1.24–3.1.55 MSI n registers; 3.1.17–3.1.19 EP_IRQ_*
dependencies: MSI_CAP/INTx config in config space; MSIn_IRQ_* enables; IRQ_EOI for EOI
Remarks: Ensure MSI_EN implies INTx disabled; clear MSIn_IRQ_STATUS and write IRQ_EOI to retire events; one legacy assert pending at a time.

feature_name: Interrupt reception and handling in RC
description: In RC mode, the controller receives legacy INTx and MSI from downstream EPs. MSI vectors (0–31) map into grouped interrupt events with enable/status/clear registers. The RC must probe downstream devices to service legacy interrupts and handle message reordering hazards. EOI acknowledges events; software should consider posted write ordering and may disable relaxed ordering for determinism.
evidence: 2.14.5 Interrupt Reception in RC Mode; Table 2-10; 3.1.24–3.1.55 MSI n registers; 3.1.15 IRQ_EOI
Remarks: Read downstream status before EOI to avoid retriggers; if ordering issues arise, clear RELAXED in DEV_STAT_CTRL and TLPCFG.

feature_name: Advanced Error Reporting (AER) and root error tracking
description: The module implements baseline and advanced PCIe error handling, including correctable, non-fatal, and fatal classes. Status/mask/severity registers exist for uncorrectable and correctable errors, and Root Complex registers capture message reception and source IDs. First Error Pointer and header logs help post-mortem analysis. Software can enable SERR signaling and per-class reporting as needed.
Remarks: Set masks and severities to policy; read and clear on service; use header logs to correlate failing TLPs; consider interrupts for AER.

feature_name: ECRC generation and checking
description: End-to-end CRC (ECRC) support can be enabled for generation and checking in the Advanced Capabilities register. When enabled, transmitted TLPs include ECRC and received TLPs are verified accordingly. A note recommends disabling ECRC if the link partner does not support it, especially in RC mode. Misconfiguration can cause false error logging and interoperability issues.
Remarks: Negotiate or validate ECRC support; if unsupported, clear ECRC bits; monitor PCIE_UNCERR[ECRC_ERR_ST] for faults.

feature_name: Device power states and ASPM (L0s/L1)
description: The device supports PCI power states D0/D1/D2/D3hot (D3cold requires power removal and re-init). Link power management supports L0s and L1 (not L2), with ASPM-controlled transitions or software-driven entry. Relationship tables define permissible device/link combinations. Registers expose PM commands and capabilities, and link control includes COMMMON_CLK and Active Link PM enables.
Remarks: Coordinate device D-states with link states; enable ASPM per system policy; heed D3cold implications (no Vaux retention).

feature_name: Clocking and SerDes PLL configuration
description: A high-quality differential reference clock feeds the SerDes PLL, which multiplies it to the internal 2.5 GHz line rate. Software programs PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL and waits for PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK] before any PCIe access. Clock control supports common-clock architectures and SSC; COMMON_CLK_CFG must be set appropriately when clocks aren’t shared to reduce training retries. Proper PLL loop bandwidth selection mitigates jitter sensitivity.
evidence: 2.2 Clock Control; 2.3 SerDes Configuration; 3.1.101–3.1.102 SERDES_CFGx; PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL/STS (Section 2.3)
Remarks: Program PLL then poll LOCK; adjust LB and MPY per refclk; set COMMON_CLK_CFG if separate clocks; avoid any access before lock.

feature_name: Initialization sequences for RC and EP
description: The user guide provides ordered steps to initialize the PCIe in RC and EP modes. Sequence covers powering domains, setting mode, SerDes programming and lock, disabling LTSSM, programming configuration, and enabling link training. RC must enumerate downstream devices; EP may gate responses with APP_RETRY_EN until ready. Following these steps ensures deterministic bring-up.
Remarks: Insert delays as required; confirm LTSSM state L0 (DEBUG0[LTSSM_STATE]=0x11) before enabling traffic; clear APP_RETRY_EN when ready.

feature_name: PIPE and PHY loopback test modes
description: The controller supports link-layer (PIPE) loopback with master/slave roles and PHY loopback for self-test without a partner. PHY loopback requires configuration of SERDES_CFGx and forcing LTSSM to POLL_ACTIVE via PL_FORCE_LINK before training. Loopback cannot be used for configuration TLPs in RC; it is intended for compliance and symbol-level testing. Proper ordering avoids sequence number mismatches.
Remarks: Set loopback before any traffic; avoid BAR0/limit overlaps; do not issue config transactions in PHY loopback.

feature_name: Port/link control, force link, ACK frequency and timers
description: Port logic registers allow forcing LTSSM states, enabling DLL, setting link width/rate hints, and transmitting custom messages. ACK/Nak frequency and training sequence counts are programmable to tune performance and compliance. Lane skew insertion and watchdog timers are available for validation. These knobs aid bring-up, debug, and marginal link tuning.
evidence: 3.9 Port Logic Registers (PL_LINK_CTRL, PL_FORCE_LINK, ACK_FREQ, LANE_SKEW, SYM_NUM)
Remarks: Use defaults for production unless characterization dictates; forcing states can disrupt traffic; document any deviations.

feature_name: Transaction constraints and alignment rules
description: Internal bus transactions are limited to 128 bytes, with stricter 120-byte limit if start address is not 8-byte aligned. Only incremental bursts are supported; fixed or wrap bursts can cause bus lockups. Write byte enables must be a single contiguous string of ones (no holes). Read responses will not be interleaved if outbound requests stay within max transaction size.
Remarks: Align buffers to 8 bytes; slice transfers to 128 bytes; enforce contiguous write strobes; avoid non-incremental bursts.

feature_name: Debug registers and LTSSM visibility
description: DEBUG0/DEBUG1 provide PIPE signals, skip status, link numbers, and current LTSSM state. The LTSSM state encoding is documented for correlation. These read-only windows help diagnose training, polarity reversal, and compliance states. Combined with header logs in AER, they provide rich observability for failure analysis.
evidence: 3.9.11 DEBUG0; 3.9.12 DEBUG1; Appendix A LTSSM States
dependencies: Accessible via Address Space 0; link must be powered and clocks present
Remarks: Poll LTSSM_STATE during bring-up; correlate with LINK_STAT_CTRL fields; use with care to avoid timing assumptions.

feature_name: Software and hot reset (RSTCMD) and wait rules
description: The module supports warm/hot reset via RSTCMD[INIT_RST], as well as system and power-on resets. After issuing hot reset, software must wait at least 100 ms before attempting PCIe transactions; link partners may need up to 1.5 s. Sticky bits in MMRs and PM logic may retain across soft reset. Proper reset sequencing ensures clean reinitialization.
Remarks: Quiesce traffic (ACT_STATUS) before reset; observe minimum delays; retrain link and re-enumerate as necessary.

feature_name: Unsupported features and limitations
description: The controller does not support using a ×2 link as two ×1 links, multiple virtual channels, multiple traffic classes, function-level reset, beacon wake, built-in hot plug, vendor messages, inbound I/O, non-incremental bursts, auxiliary power retention for D3cold, or L2 link state. These constraints bound system design choices. Awareness prevents unsupported configurations. Documented omissions guide integration decisions.
dependencies: System architecture; board design; system power policy
Remarks: Plan for external hot-plug and wake mechanisms if needed; avoid IO inbound usage; ensure power states and VC/TC counts match needs.

feature_name: PCIe message transmit/receive and custom messages
description: The core supports PCIe messages for both transmit and receive. A programmable Other Message Register can inject specific messages when requested via PL_LINK_CTRL. Message-based interrupts and PM events use these mechanisms. This capability enables standards-compliant control signaling over the link.
dependencies: PL_OMSG payload; OMSG_REQ trigger; message routing
Remarks: Use only standard-defined message types; vendor-defined messages are not supported and are dropped by default.

feature_name: Completion timeout capabilities and control
description: Device Capabilities 2 expose supported completion timeout ranges and whether disable is supported. Software sets the operational timeout in Device Status/Control 2, with guidance to avoid values under 10 ms. These policies affect requester robustness against missing completions. Proper configuration balances responsiveness and false timeout risk.
Remarks: Use default 50 ms–50 s where appropriate; avoid too-short windows; tune per system latency characteristics.

feature_name: Security mechanisms (not specified)
description: The specification does not describe dedicated security features such as link encryption, access control lists, or authentication. Operation relies on standard PCIe mechanisms (e.g., BAR filtering, bus mastering enables) and error detection/reporting for reliability, not confidentiality. Vendor-defined messages are explicitly unsupported. System-level security must be provided outside the PCIe controller. Treat security posture as platform-dependent.
evidence: No explicit security section; 1.3 Features (no vendor messaging)
dependencies: System IOMMU/ACLs; RC policy; OS drivers
Remarks: Harden at system level (IOMMU, privilege checks); restrict bus mastering to trusted devices; review BAR exposure.

feature_name: Power domain control and module state transitions
description: The PCIe power domain can be turned off and on via the device PSC, which resets the module to initial state upon re-enable. All inbound and outbound traffic must be stopped beforehand to avoid hangs. After power-up, link training must be restarted and configuration re-applied. There is no extra delay beyond PSC handshakes required by the module itself.
evidence: 2.13.3 Power Domain and Module State Transitions Considerations
Remarks: Quiesce using ACT_STATUS; coordinate with link partner; re-run full init and enumeration after power cycling the domain.}]}] }  }`}/>```
